# Chapter 03

[k6q3m8v1] Flynn woke before sunrise because the desert woke him. The trailer they’d given him smelled of old rubber and solvent and a sweetness that might have been spilled fuel or might have been mice. He lay still for a moment listening to the Compound breathe—generator thudding, wind worrying at tarps, a distant clink of metal like someone speaking in their sleep.

[1w9n0c7h] His phone was gone, sealed in Diesel’s little box. The absence sat in his pocket like a missing tooth. He kept reaching for it anyway, thumb brushing fabric, his body asking for the old reassurance of a screen.

[9t2k5m0s] Outside, the sky was pale as bone. Diesel was already up. She stood under the dead gas station roof with a mug of something black and bitter, watching the horizon as if expecting it to move.

[7x1p6n4q] “You hungry?” she asked.

[0m8v2k1d] Flynn nodded, though hunger wasn’t the right word. He felt hollow, like his insides had been scooped out and replaced with anticipation.

[3h6m0p9c] Diesel pointed at a cooler. Inside were tortillas, a block of cheese, a jar of pickled peppers. Nothing packaged, nothing branded. Flynn ate standing up, leaning on a pump that would never pump again.

[5n2d7k0m] Cage appeared when the sun cleared the ridge, like he’d been summoned by light. He walked slow, stretching his shoulders, looking bored and dangerous. “Day one,” he said to Flynn. “Don’t die.”

[8c0x7m2p] “He can’t,” Birdie said, popping up from behind a stack of tires. He grinned. “Not legally.”

[2p9m1v6k] Cage’s mouth twitched. “Don’t get revived,” he amended, and the smile he offered after was thin enough to cut.

[6r1k9d3x] Don didn’t look up from the engine he was rebuilding. He just slid a wrench across the workbench toward Flynn without turning. The gesture was simple. Use this. Earn your air.

[4v7m0q2n] Flynn picked it up. It was heavier than it looked, scarred, warm from someone else’s hand. The weight felt like permission.

[q3m6n1x8] Diesel led him behind the trailers to a fenced corner that stank of grease. Barrels sat in the shade, capped tight. A cracked plastic tote held pale sludge. A small still made from scavenged tubing rose from a propane burner that had been modified to burn something else.

[1c5p8v0d] “Fuel,” Diesel said. She said it like other people said *food.*

[7m0k2x9r] Flynn stared. “That’s… vegetable oil?”

[0d9p3n6h] “Used,” Diesel corrected. “From restaurants. From the city if Birdie’s lucky. From wherever people still fry things and pretend the world hasn’t ended.”

[2x7h0m1p] Birdie lifted a bucket like a prize. The oil inside was dark and smelled like old onions. “This one’s from a place that makes churros,” he said. “You can tell because it’s sadder.”

[9k1m6p0x] Cage leaned on a post and watched Flynn’s face. “You thought we had a gas station out back?” he said.

[3p0x8m4v] “I didn’t know what I thought,” Flynn admitted.

[6h2n7c1m] Diesel crouched and opened a barrel. The smell hit Flynn hard—rancid, metallic. She didn’t flinch. “We filter. We heat. We split it. We take the water out. We take the food out. We make something an engine will accept,” she said. “It’s not pretty. It’s work.”

[8m4v1k0s] “Is it… safe?” Flynn asked, and regretted it the instant the word left his mouth.

[0k7m2p9d] Cage laughed once. “Listen to you,” he said. “Like a pod manual.”

[5x0n6m2q] Diesel’s eyes flicked to Cage, then back to Flynn. “Nothing here is safe,” she said. “But it’s controlled. There’s a difference. You keep your hands out of the wrong places. You don’t smoke. You don’t get lazy.”

[1n9c0x6h] She showed him the filter rig: cloth stretched tight over a frame, the oil poured through slow as confession. A second stage with finer mesh. A third with charcoal. Everything made from something else. Everything designed to keep them alive.

[7q2m6p0x] “Why not buy it?” Flynn asked.

[3m1d8k0v] Birdie snorted. “Because that’s a felony.”

[9v0k3n2c] Diesel nodded. “Because pumps report volumes. Because licenses talk. Because the state wants your money and your obedience. They don’t want you driving.”

[6p2x9m0k] Cage’s voice drifted in, casual. “They want you transported.”

[0x6h1m9p] Flynn felt a chill despite the heat. “How do you avoid detection?”

[8n0m3p7d] Diesel pointed at the horizon where the solar farms began, a glittering grid. “Out here the sensors are fewer. They watch the highways. They watch the city edges. They don’t watch broken places.” She wiped her hands on a rag. “We move small. We never do the same thing twice. We don’t light fires where people can see smoke. We don’t buy parts with names. We don’t keep our implants on.”

[2m7k0v9x] Cage tilted his head. “And we don’t panic,” he added. “Panic makes you sloppy. Sloppy makes you dead.”

[5d1m8p0q] Flynn nodded like he understood. He didn’t. Not yet. He could feel the edges of the life he’d left behind, a life where the rules were posted and the penalties were mostly financial. Here the penalties were bodies.

[t7m0x2n9] Birdie touched Flynn’s elbow. “Come on,” he whispered, like they were sneaking out of school. “You gotta see the boneyard.”

[0p9n4k2x] Diesel started to object, then paused. Cage had already turned away, bored with fuel. Don kept working. Wrench watched from the shade, eyes on Flynn like he was watching a bird decide whether to fly.

[6k2m0v8p] “Stay in sight,” Diesel said. It was both permission and threat.

[3x0m7p1n] Birdie led Flynn past the trailers and out along a wash where the sand held the tracks of coyotes and a single bike tire. They walked for ten minutes and the desert opened into a wide, shallow basin.

[9m1k6x0d] The boneyard lay there like a graveyard that had been robbed. Rows of dead aircraft fuselages sat half-buried, white skins peeled back by sun. Between them, under tarps and rusted scaffolds, were cars. Thousands of them. Bodies without hearts, hearts without bodies.

[2h7m0p9c] Flynn stopped without meaning to. His throat tightened. In the city, old cars existed as museum pieces behind glass. Here they lay open to the weather, sacrificial, waiting.

[5p0n6m1x] Birdie walked with reverence. He stepped carefully between a pair of fenders as if crossing a church aisle. “They bring them here after confiscation,” he said softly. “Or they used to. Now they shred most of them. But this place… this place got forgotten.”

[0n2m8k6v] Flynn ran his hand along a door panel. The paint had blistered. Under it, the metal was pitted but solid. The touch sent a small shock through him, like touching a sleeping animal.

[7m9c1x0p] “It’s like they’re all… dead,” Flynn said.

[3k0m8p2d] “Not dead,” Birdie corrected. “Sleeping. Waiting for someone to remember them.”

[9x2m0v6n] They ducked under a tarp and came into shadow. Parts hung from hooks: carburetors, distributors, hoses, gaskets wrapped in wax paper like relics. A hand-painted sign read: **TAKE ONLY WHAT YOU CAN USE.**

[6v1m7p0x] Wrench was there, surprising Flynn. He sat on an overturned crate with a cigarette that wasn’t lit, rolling it between his fingers like a thought he couldn’t finish. “Birdie dragging you through the holy place?” he asked.

[0m6p2n9k] Birdie grinned. “He needs to see what’s real.”

[8p0x3m1d] Wrench looked at Flynn. Up close, he seemed older than he’d looked by the fire. Not in years. In wear. “You ever rebuild anything?” he asked.

[2n9m0k7x] “No,” Flynn said, then added quickly, “not like this.”

[5m1x7p0d] Wrench nodded. He reached up and took a carburetor from a hook. It was small, heavy, a compact geometry of metal and screws and passages. He set it in Flynn’s hands.

[0x2m8p6n] Flynn felt the cold weight. He could see where fuel had once flowed through it, leaving varnish like old blood. He turned it over slowly, afraid of dropping it, afraid of proving he didn’t deserve to touch it.

[9p1m0v7k] “That’s a Quadrajet,” Wrench said. “Came off a ’78. Not special. Special’s not the point.” He tapped the body with one knuckle. “It’s honest. You can see what it wants.”

[3m7k0p2x] “How do you make it work again?” Flynn asked.

[6x0m1p9d] Wrench’s mouth curved in something like a smile. “You clean it. You listen. You don’t force it. Diesel will teach you. Cage will tell you you’re doing it wrong no matter what you do.”

[0k3m8p2v] “Why?” Flynn asked.

[7p2m0n9x] Wrench looked past him toward the sunlit rows. “Because if he admits you can learn, he has to admit you can leave,” he said. The words came out quiet, like he hadn’t meant to say them at all.

[2m0x6p8n] Birdie’s grin faded. He looked away.

[9n1m7p0x] Flynn felt a small, sharp fear. He’d thought the danger was outside the fence. He realized it could be inside it too.

[5p2m0k8d] “Come on,” Birdie said quickly, forcing brightness back into his voice. “Let’s get you greasy. Diesel’s gonna start yelling.”

[0v8m2p0x] Back at the Compound the heat had climbed. Diesel had spread tools on a table under the roof. Don’s engine sat open like a patient. Cage lounged in a chair with his boots on another chair, watching the work the way a king watched labor.

[6m0k9p1x] Diesel set the Quadrajet on the table. “We’re going to rebuild it,” she said. “Not because we need this one. Because you need to learn what fuel does when it meets air.”

[3p1m0x7d] Flynn nodded and tried to keep his hands steady.

[9x0m6p2n] Diesel pulled the carb apart with efficient violence. Screws out. Plates off. Float removed. She laid each piece in order like she was arranging bones. “Every channel matters,” she said. “You clog one, you run lean, you burn valves. You run rich, you foul plugs, you lose power. You want power, you have to respect the boring parts.”

[2k8m0p7x] Cage leaned forward. “Or you can just floor it and pray,” he said.

[0n6m2x9p] Diesel didn’t look at him. “That’s your religion,” she said.

[7m0p1x8d] Flynn cleaned the pieces in solvent, watching brown streaks dissolve. The smell stung his eyes. He worked slowly, terrified of missing something invisible.

[3x2m0p9n] Don came over once, wiped his hands, and adjusted Flynn’s grip on a screwdriver. No words. Just a correction that saved Flynn from stripping a thread.

[9m0k2p7x] Birdie hovered behind Flynn’s shoulder, offering commentary like a sportscaster. “That’s the needle. That’s the seat. That’s where it leaks if you’re dumb.”

[2p0m8k6d] “I’m not dumb,” Flynn said, surprised by his own defensiveness.

[0x7m1p9n] Cage’s laugh was soft. “We’ll see,” he said.

[6n2m0p8x] Flynn assembled the carb again, hands shaking less now. He set the float. He checked the gasket surfaces. Diesel watched without hovering. Her silence was a kind of test.

[3m0k7p2d] When he tightened the last screw, Diesel reached in and twisted the throttle linkage. It moved smooth. She nodded once. “It’ll work,” she said.

[9p2m0x6n] Flynn realized he’d been holding his breath. He let it out and felt something loosen in his chest.

[0m8p1x7d] Wrench clapped him once on the shoulder as he passed, light as dust. “See?” he said. “You can learn.”

[6x0m2p9n] Cage stood up, stretching. “Congratulations,” he said, like the word tasted sour. “You rebuilt a thing. Tomorrow you’ll rebuild a thing that can kill you.”

[3p0m8k1x] Flynn looked at the carburetor on the table. It was still just metal. But it felt different now, as if he’d earned the right to imagine fuel running through it again, air and fire becoming motion.

[9m1x0p7d] The sun dropped toward the horizon. The desert cooled by degrees. Diesel covered the tools. Don went back to his engine. Birdie ran off to wherever he kept his restlessness.

[0k2m8p6x] Flynn sat alone under the roof and watched the shadows lengthen across the dead pumps. He thought of the city again—its smooth lights, its quiet pods sliding along their lanes—and the thought no longer made him feel safe. It made him feel trapped.

[6p0m1x9d] Cage’s voice came from behind him, low, almost kind. “You feel it yet?”

[3m8p0k2n] Flynn didn’t turn. “Feel what?”

[9x0m6p1d] “That you can do something real,” Cage said. “That you can break something and fix it. That consequences exist.”

[2m1x8p0d] Flynn stared out at the desert until the heat shimmer became dusk. “Yeah,” he said. “I feel it.”

[0p7m0k9x] Cage was quiet for a moment. Then, like an afterthought, he said, “Good. Hold on to it. The world will try to take it back.”
